channel.title: NYT > Climate and Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Administration Backs Off Plan to End Ocean Monitoring System
item.description: The reversal comes after the Senate passed a bipartisan bill on Wednesday to block the removal of deep-sea monitoring instruments.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Darlene Trew Crist/WHOI, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A data-collecting buoy off the Carolina coast. The National Science Foundation was planning to begin removing hundreds of ocean instruments that measure ocean data this month.

item.title: New Coral Study Identifies Areas Where Reefs Are Hanging On
item.description: New research has identified areas around the world where cooler currents and other favorable conditions are helping to protect coral from the worst effects of global warming.
item.dc:creator: Katharine Houreld

item.title: D.O.J. Seeks to Halt Pollution Lawsuit Against Elon Musk’s Data Center
item.description: The department cited national security concerns, saying Elon Musk’s company had played a crucial role in the Iran war. It also argued it has the authority to stop environmental lawsuits brought by citizens.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: National Assn for the Advancement of Colored People
item.media:credit: Brad J. Vest for The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction at xAI’s power plant in Southaven, Miss., in February. The NAACP has sued the company over air pollution from generators there.

item.title: Algae Is Turning the Reflecting Pool Green. Again.
item.description: Algal blooms have hit the site, between the Lincoln Memorial and the Washington Monument, after a $14.2 million repair project.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:description: The green water didn't bother some visitors.

item.title: Tensions Are Rising Among States That Rely on the Colorado River
item.description: A prolonged drought means the nation’s largest reservoirs are dwindling, and litigation over access to water could lie ahead.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Nina Riggio for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Upper Colorado River on the Grand Canyon last month. About 40 million people and 5.5 million acres of cropland depend on the Colorado for drinking water and irrigation.

item.title: Mines, Logistics and Deep Uncertainty Threaten a Middle East Oil Rebound
item.description: More oil is getting out of the Persian Gulf, but the region’s producers are looking for signs that it is safe as they ramp up plans for alternative routes.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels, including oil tankers, anchored this week in the waters off Muscat, Oman, near the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: A Humble 3-Wheel Electric Vehicle Lands Toyota in Federal Court
item.description: A lawsuit filed in California claims the automaker’s philanthropic arm stole technology intended to help poor farmers, but it is not clear to what end.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:credit: Cynthia R. Matonhodze for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mobility for Africa developed a three-wheeled electric vehicle that rural residents of Zimbabwe use to transport produce and other goods to market.

item.title: Extreme Heat Warnings Issued Across Europe: What to Know
item.description: Unusually high temperatures are expected across parts of Western Europe into early next week, weather agencies said.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel

item.title: Is Climate Change Supercharging El Niño?
item.description: As a new, potentially record-breaking El Niño begins, researchers are vigorously debating whether climate change is driving the phenomenon’s intensity.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:description: Fighting a wildfire in South Sumatra, Indonesia, during an El Niño-exacerbated dry season in 2023.

item.title: Have You Bought a Tesla Recently? We Want to Hear From You.
item.description: Tesla sales are rebounding in Europe even though many people have negative feelings about Elon Musk, the company’s chief executive. We want to know why.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:description: Tesla’s car sales took a big hit in Europe last year, in part because many people were turned off by the political views of the company’s chief executive, Elon Musk.

item.title: The Shifting Politics of Climate Change
item.description: A new poll suggests Republicans may be more movable on climate change than previously thought, plus more climate news.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: While words and phrases like “decarbonization” and climate “crisis” were shown to be ineffective, messages saying that pollution led to rising costs and extreme weather were more likely to persuade U.S. voters.

item.title: Amazon Retaliated Against Workers Who Supported Regulating Data Centers, Complaint Says
item.description: The employees encouraged limits on the complexes in a series of hearings in the tech giant’s hometown, Seattle.
item.dc:creator: Karen Weise
item.media:credit: Jovelle Tamayo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Amazon’s campus in Seattle, Wash.

item.title: Tropical Storm Arthur Prompts Rare Forecast Warning for the Southeast
item.description: The remnants of Tropical Storm Arthur are raising the risk of flooding across the Gulf Coast.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:description: Floodwaters rose in Galveston, Texas, on Wednesday.

item.title: U.S. Will Waive Oil Sanctions That Have Long Crimped Iran
item.description: The preliminary U.S.-Iran deal temporarily waives restrictions that have limited the country’s oil sales and how much money it has been able to make from those exports.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil facility on Kharg Island, Iran, in 2017.

item.title: Trump Administration to Pay $765 Million to Cancel 4 More Wind Projects
item.description: It’s the third such deal the Interior Department has struck to pay firms to abandon plans for offshore turbines, spending roughly $2.5 billion to get companies to abandon their wind projects.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:description: The Trump administration has now struck deals energy developers totaling more than $2.5 billion to cancel offshore wind leases.

item.title: Trump’s FEMA Nominee Calls Staff Cuts a ‘Challenge’ for Disaster Agency
item.description: Cameron Hamilton, who briefly led the agency on an acting basis last year but was fired for contradicting the president, also said he would get money out to states faster.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.category: Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs
item.media:description: Cameron Hamilton, President Trump’s FEMA nominee, testifying before the Senate Homeland Security Committee on Wednesday.

item.title: Elon Musk’s Next Move May Be a Mega-Merger of SpaceX and Tesla
item.description: Some shareholders might object, but there is little they could do, legal experts say.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing and Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Tesla has sold car and batteries to SpaceX, and the two companies are jointly working on chips and software.

item.title: How a Deal to End the War in Iran Could Affect Energy Prices
item.description: The latest on a tentative deal to end the war in Iran, the Justice Department tries to stop a lawsuit against Elon Musk’s A.I. company and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
National Assn for the Advancement of Colored People
item.media:description: Even if the hundreds of tankers stuck in the Persian Gulf start moving soon, it will take weeks or months for them to reach their destinations.

item.title: Gas Prices May Remain High Despite US-Iran Deal
item.description: The preliminary agreement may not have an immediate effect on prices at the pump. Damaged infrastructure and risky transport could keep costs up.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Gas prices have soared since the start of the war with Iran.

item.title: Deal to Reopen Hormuz Kicks Off Long Effort to Ease Energy Crisis
item.description: The pace of the recovery will depend on how confident companies are that the deal between the United States and Iran will hold and be extended.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz this month.

item.title: China Moves the Price of Oil, Even When It Buys Less
item.description: China is the world’s largest purchaser of oil. But three months after the war with Iran began, it cut its imports, cushioning the global market.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Alexandra Stevenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil refinery in Nanjing in China’s Jiangsu Province.

item.title: In Alabama, Opposition to Renewable Solar Energy Joins a Data Center Battle
item.description: Tuesday’s runoff for a slot on the Alabama Public Service Commission has a familiar ring to it, with talk of data centers and electricity costs. But in a southern twist, solar power has joined the list of villains.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen and Audra Melton
item.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Alabama Public Service Commission has suddenly become a hot-button issue ahead of Tuesday’s runoff primaries.

item.title: The Secrets of a Soccer-Turf Master
item.description: John Sorochan, a turf scientist at the University of Tennessee, has led the yearslong, multimillion-dollar effort to develop perfect playing fields for the 2026 World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Alan Burdick

item.title: The Scientific Quest for Perfect World Cup Pitch
item.description: Every match must be played on natural grass that gives players as consistent a surface as possible, no matter the venue. Cue the years of sod studies.
item.dc:creator: Alan Burdick

item.title: Judge Blocks National Parks From Removing ‘Negative’ Signs and Depictions of Slavery
item.description: The ruling accused the Trump administration of engaging in censorship by taking down materials at parks across the country.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times
item.media:description: An outdoor exhibit on slavery at Independence National Historical Park in Philadelphia, seen last year, was taken down in January.

item.title: Days of Dangerous Heat Is Expected in the Pacific Northwest
item.description: Portland could climb near 100 degrees this weekend, forecasters said. Little relief is expected until next week.
item.dc:creator: Erin McCann
item.media:description: Many homes in the Pacific Northwest lack air-conditioning.

item.title: Renewable Groups Ask Courts to End Pentagon’s ‘Total Halt’ of Wind Power
item.description: More than 100 planned wind farms in 21 states are now stalled indefinitely as the Pentagon delays military reviews once seen as routine.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Brandon Thibodeaux for The New York Times
item.media:description: The freeze affects 41 wind projects in Texas, which produces more wind power than any other state in the U.S., as well as projects in Colorado, Illinois, Iowa, Kansas, New Mexico, Oregon and Washington.

item.title: The World Is Draining Oil Reserves, Raising Pressure for a Peace Deal
item.description: The amount of oil and fuel stored by businesses and governments has fallen sharply since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Lazaro Gamio
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: She Used A.I. to Create Better Forecasts for Extreme Weather. Then Her Funding Was Cut.
item.description: Amy McGovern was the director of an A.I. institute dedicated to weather forecasting — until the National Science Foundation ended its funding.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Brett Deering for The New York Times

item.title: How Should Democrats Talk About Climate Change?
item.description: A big new milestone for solar power, a shift in Democratic messaging and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Tim Gruber for The New York Times
item.media:description: Solar power supplied 12.8 percent of U.S. electricity in May, edging out coal, which supplied just 12.2 percent.

item.title: Scientists Measure Earth’s Vast Underground Fungal Webs
item.description: With machine learning and a high-resolution imaging robot, scientists measured and mapped the extent of Earth’s carbon circulatory system.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes

item.title: NOAA Issues El Nino Advisory
item.description: The global weather pattern threatens to worsen floods and heat waves already intensifying due to climate change. But it may also mean fewer hurricanes.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:description: Drought-stressed wheat in a field in Kansas last month.

item.title: Hazardous Temperatures Forecast for Many World Cup Games
item.description: Many of the tournament’s host cities risk hitting dangerous heat levels this year, raising questions about the safety of players and fans.
item.dc:creator: Quinn Glabicki
item.media:description: Thiago Santos, a player with Brazil’s Fluminense team, during a cooling break at a match in New Jersey last July.

item.title: Millions Across the Midwest and Northeast Face Severe Storms on Thursday
item.description: For the second day in a row, Chicago is in the bull’s-eye of the areas facing severe weather on Thursday, but the storm forecast is widespread.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones

item.title: Democrats Once Vowed to Stop Oil and Gas. Now They’re Not So Sure.
item.description: As the midterm elections approach, many leading Democrats are rethinking their approach to climate change.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Brad Plumer
item.media:description: A talk on rising energy costs on Capitol Hill hosted by Democrats, from left, Sen. Martin Heinrich of New Mexico, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer and Sen. Sheldon Whitehouse of Rhode Island, in March.

item.title: In the Fastest-Warming City in the U.S., These Artists See Hope
item.description: At the Nevada Museum of Art, five examples of artist-activists shining a light on the Great Basin Desert and beyond.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Griffin

item.title: Lee Raymond, Who Created Global Oil Behemoth Exxon Mobil, Dies at 87
item.description: He oversaw Exxon’s acquisition of a rival, cut costs relentlessly and denied the scientific consensus on climate change.
item.dc:creator: James R. Hagerty
item.media:description: Lee Raymond, the chairman and chief executive of Exxon Mobil Corp., at a news conference in 2005. A former high school debating champion, he was known for making withering remarks to those who challenged him.

item.title: Heat in the Northeast Thursday and Friday Brings the Threat of Dangerous Storms
item.description: Soupy, hot weather will settle into the Northeast on Thursday and Friday, bringing with it the threat of dangerous storms.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: A security guard in New York cooling down during a hot day last summer. This week will bring the year’s first widespread dose of summerlike heat and humidity to the city.

item.title: Indonesia Landslides Devastated Endangered Orangutans, Study Finds
item.description: More than 5 percent of the species is estimated to have been lost when a climate-fueled storm unleashed torrents of water, mud and debris.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn

item.title: G.M. Plans to Develop Energy Storage Batteries as E.V. Sales Flag
item.description: The carmaker will follow Tesla in making components for large batteries used by electric utilities, data centers and other businesses to handle fluctuations in power supply and demand.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:description: The main active ingredient in the storage batteries that General Motors plans to produce is sodium, which is cheaper and easier to process than lithium.

item.title: The Movement to Stop Data Centers
item.description: What to know about the local backlash against the booming data center industry, the era of me-first energy and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:credit: Will Warasila for The New York Times
item.media:description: Opposition to a data center in April in Broadview, Mont.

item.title: The Iran War is Forcing Energy-Importing Countries to Turn Inward
item.description: The Iran war is pushing countries to prioritize domestic energy in order to protect themselves from volatile oil and natural gas markets.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jes Aznar for The New York Times
item.media:description: The president of Solaric, a rooftop-solar installer based near Manila, said people were now seeing the technology as “a way to survive this new age.”
